coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-modified silver nanoparticles as a new colorimetric sensor for selective determination of cupric ions
coredata.dc:creator: Beiraghi, Asadollah
Najibi-Gehraz, Seyed Ali
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dots-modified silver nanoparticles (CDs-Ag NPs) were successfully prepared by reducing AgNO3 in the presence of carbon dots (CDs). The prepared CDs-Ag NPs were characterized by using UV–vis spectroscopy, IR spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and dynamic light scattering (DLS). Aqueous dispersions of the CDs-Ag NPs showed strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption band at 430nm. A simple, sensitive, and selective colorimetric Cu2+ sensor was developed on the basis of the strong interaction of functional groups of the CDs incorporated in CDs-Ag NPs with Cu+2 cations which in turn resulted in the aggregation of the nanoparticles and manifested itself in the change of color of CDs-Ag NPs dispersion from orange to red-brown. The main factors affecting the Cu2+ detection, including pH, concentration of CDs, ionic strength, and the interaction time, were optimized for finding out the best conditions for determination of Cu2+. Under the optimum conditions, the analytical response (A525/A430) was linear over the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.3–8μM (r 2 =0.999) and a detection limit of 0.037μM was obtained. The method was successfully used for detection of Cu2+ in tap and river water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ag NPs
CDs-Ag NPs
Colorimetric sensor
Copper

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for spectrophotometric determination of Cu2+
Characterization of the synthesized CDs-Ag NPs
Metal ion sensing capability of CDs-Ag NPs
Effect of CDs concentration used in CDs-Ag NPs synthesis
Quantification of Cu2+ using CDs-Ag NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectra and visual appearance of the CDs, Ag NPs, and CDs-Ag NPs.

(A) FT-IR spectrum of the prepared CDs. The inset shows the schematic representation of the synthesized CDs with its recognized surface functional groups. (B) FT-IR spectra of CDs-Ag NPs. Inset: Schematic representation of the synthesized CDs-Ag NPs.
DLS of bare Ag NPs (A) and CDs-Ag NPs (B). TEM images of Ag NPs (C) and CDs-Ag NPs (D).

UV–vis absorption spectra (A) and Photographic image (B) of CDs-Ag NPs (0.02mg/ml) in the presence of 10μM different metal ions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, K+, Na+, and Fe3+).
Schematic illustration of Cu2+ recognition mechanism by the colorimetric sensor.
(A) TEM image and (B) DLS measurement of CDs-Ag NPs upon addition of 10μM Cu2+.
Absorption spectra of CDs-Ag NPs at different pH levels. Inset displays the absorption values (A430) versus pH of the dispersion.
UV–vis spectral (A) and color (B) change of CDs-Ag NPs dispersion (0.02mg/ml) upon the addition of different concentration of Cu2+ under the optimum conditions.
The calibration graph for the quantification of Cu2+ using CDs-Ag NPs.
Comparison of the proposed method with some other NPs-based previously reported methods for the detection of Cu2+.
Tolerance ratio of the interfering ions in quantification of Cu2+ (5μM).
Determination of Cu2+ in real water samples by using the proposed colorimetric sensor.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-modified silver nanoparticles as a new colorimetric sensor for selective determination of cupric ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Mofatteh Ave., No.49, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon dots-modified silver nanoparticles (CDs-Ag NPs) were successfully prepared by reducing AgNO3 in the presence of carbon dots (CDs). The prepared CDs-Ag NPs were characterized by using UV–vis spectroscopy, IR spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and dynamic light scattering (DLS). Aqueous dispersions of the CDs-Ag NPs showed strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption band at 430nm. A simple, sensitive, and selective colorimetric Cu2+ sensor was developed on the basis of the strong interaction of functional groups of the CDs incorporated in CDs-Ag NPs with Cu+2 cations which in turn resulted in the aggregation of the nanoparticles and manifested itself in the change of color of CDs-Ag NPs dispersion from orange to red-brown. The main factors affecting the Cu2+ detection, including pH, concentration of CDs, ionic strength, and the interaction time, were optimized for finding out the best conditions for determination of Cu2+. Under the optimum conditions, the analytical response (A525/A430) was linear over the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.3–8μM (r 2 =0.999) and a detection limit of 0.037μM was obtained. The method was successfully used for detection of Cu2+ in tap and river water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing efficient analysis systems for the determination of heavy metal ions has emerged as an important field of research because of the profound environmental and biological impacts imposed by these chemical species. There is a great deal of variety in the sources of these contaminants, among which coal, paper, natural gas, electronic, and chlor-alkali industries can be mentioned [1]. It is well proved that trace amounts of heavy metal ions, which can enter into the body through various routes, have toxic effects on human body and can cause many types of diseases [2]. Cu2+ cation is well-known for being the third essential transitional metal in the human body in terms of abundance [3]. It is of great importance in a variety of metalloenzymes, such as cytochrome oxidase, superoxide dismutase, and tyrosinase [4,5]. However, high levels of Cu2+ may give rise to some serious problems, like hypertension, increased respiratory rates, liver and kidney damage, and disturbance in the central nervous system [6,7]. Therefore developing a simple, selective, and sensitive method for measuring trace amounts of cupric ion in drinking and environmental water samples is of great importance. A variety of techniques such as molecular fluorescence spectroscopy [8,9], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [10,11], chemiluminescence [12], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [13], ion chromatography [14], and electrochemical methods [15] have been used for the determination of Cu2+ in various samples. These methods, however, suffer from one or more of disadvantages such as being expensive, time-consuming, and requiring sophisticated equipment which limits their widespread applications.

Besides the molecular colorimetric probes that have been used for many years for determining different types of analytes including metal ions [16], nowadays gold nanoparticles-based (Au NPs-based) and silver nanoparticles-based (Ag NPs-based) colorimetric sensors have attracted extensive attention in chemical, biological, and environmental analysis due to their simplicity, low cost, and high sensitivity [17]. The color of a dispersion of Au NPs or Ag NPs depends on the size of the NPs and the distance between them. Hence, the color of the well-dispersed Ag NPs or Au NPs is completely different from that of the corresponding highly aggregated NPs. Based on the mentioned changes in the color and UV–vis spectrum of these NPs, a wide range of colorimetric sensors have been proposed for the selective detection of various organic and inorganic species. Regarding all of these sensors, the capping agent determines the surface chemistry of the NPs which plays a crucial role in the selectivity and sensitivity of the method. In this regard, some colorimetric sensors based on Ag NPs and Au NPs capped with various molecules, such as glutathione, folic acid, N-acetyl-l-cysteine, l-cysteine, thiomalic acid, 5-sulfosalicylic acid, and azacrown ether have been developed for the selective determination of Al3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ ions in various water samples [18–24].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a newly discovered attractive family of carbon-based nanomaterials [25] with a particle size of less than 10nm that have gained extensive research interests due to their fascinating properties such as desirable biocompatibility, excellent photostability, low toxicity, great water solubility, simple preparation, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity to target analytes [26]. Depending on the method and precursors used in their synthesis, various types of CDs with different surface functional groups have been prepared [27,28]. Up to now, in conjunction with CDs, luminescence-based detection is the most frequently used technique that have been applied to quantify various metal ions such as Hg2+ [29,30], Fe3+ [31,32], Ag+ [33,34], Cu2+, Co2+, Au3+ and Pb2+ [35–38] with CDs used as the luminescent probe. Here, we have used citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors to synthesize n-doped CDs with surface functional groups such as NH2, OH and COOH.

Here we report a highly selective, sensitive, and rapid method based on designing a new colorimetric sensor for the spectrophotometric determination of Cu2+ ions using carbon dots-modified silver nanoparticles (CDs-Ag NPs). To the best of our knowledge no colorimetric sensor using CDs-Ag NPs has been reported in the literature for monitoring Cu2+. Meanwhile, for the first time, we have integrated the excellent metal recognition ability of CDs with simplicity and sensitivity of the Ag NPs-based colorimetry to design this sensitive colorimetric sensor for the detection of Cu2+ in various water samples.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, sodium borohydride, AgNO3, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, HgCl2, MnCl2·4H2O, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(CH3COO)2, AlCl3, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, K2CrO4, KCl, NaCl, FeCl3·3H2O, HCl, and NaOH were obtained from Merck or Sigma-Aldrich and were used as received without further purification. All solutions were prepared with double-distilled, deionized water. Amicon Ultra-15 Centrifugal Filter Units (3KDa, 15-ml) (Millipore) was used for purification of CDs. Standard Cu2+ solutions were prepared by stepwise dilution of the stock 1mM Cu2+ solution just before use.

UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired using a double-beam in-time spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, model Lambda 25, U.S.A.) with 1cm quartz cell. FT-IR spectra were obtained on a Perkin-Elmer RXI spectrometer using KBr disks. TEM measurement was made using a Philips CM20 TEM equipment operated at 200kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) data were obtained by Nanotrac Wave (Microtrac, San Diego, CA) at room temperature. All pH measurement were performed using a Metrohm 827pH meter.

The CDs were synthesized according to a previously reported method [39]. For this purpose, 2g citric acid and 390μl ethylenediamine were mixed with 5ml of water in a beaker to form a clear solution. The obtained mixture was then put into a 680W domestic microwave oven for 5min which resulted in the formation of a red-brown foamy solid. It was dissolved in double-distilled water and purified by using centrifugal filter unit (3000Da).

CDs-modified Ag NPs were prepared by reducing AgNO3 with NaBH4 in the presence of CDs. Briefly; 1ml of AgNO3 (20mM) solution was added to a 200ml reaction flask containing 98ml CDs solution (0.05mgml−1) with pH 7. After stirring for 30min, 1ml of freshly prepared NaBH4 solution (20mM) was added dropwise to the mixture and the reaction was conducted under vigorous stirring at room temperature. After 2h, the obtained orange solution indicated the formation of CDs-Ag NPs nanocomposite. For purification, the crude CDs-Ag NPs solution was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 30min. Then, the precipitate was separated from the supernatant and was re-dispersed in double-distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for spectrophotometric determination of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for spectrophotometric determination of Cu2+ was as follows. An aliquot (100μl) of Cu2+ solution of desired concentration (0.3–8μM) was introduced into a sample vial containing 3ml of 0.02mg/ml CDs-Ag NPs dispersion. After 10min (when visual changes in the color of the vial content appeared), the absorption spectrum of the final mixture was recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The ratio of absorbance values at 525nm and 430nm (A525/A430) was used to quantify the concentration of Cu2+ in the sample.

The as-prepared CDs were characterized by UV–vis and FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 1 (A) shows the UV–vis spectrum of the CDs. The peak located at ∼340nm can be assigned to the n → π* electronic transition of carbonyl and amino functional groups [40], which is characteristic of n-doped CDs.

To investigate the surface functional groups, dried powder of the CDs was analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 A). The broad peak appeared at 3277cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds [41]. The peaks centered at 2944, 1705, and 1663cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH, CO, and CN bonds, respectively [41]. The band at 1566cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC, whereas the peaks at 1434, 1399, 1285, and 1183cm −1 were corresponded to the stretching vibrations of (COO−), CN, aromatic CNH, and CO, respectively [42]. All results revealed that there are plenty of oxygen containing functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl) and amino groups on the surface of CDs, which are suitable for effective interaction with metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized CDs-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 1(B), bare Ag NPs show SPR absorption band at ∼390nm, whereas in the case of CDs-Ag NPs, the characteristic SPR absorption band of silver nanoparticles can be observed at 430nm (Fig. 1C). The red shift observed in the SPR peak suggested that the CDs were successfully attached to the Ag NPs. Moreover, the broadening of the SPR peak in CDs-Ag NPs can be attributed to the increase in size of the Ag NPs due to their interaction with CDs. Further investigation was performed by FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs-Ag NPs (Fig. 2B) showed absorption bands at 1228, 1431, 1548, 1653, 2938, and 3282

cm−1corresponding to CO, COO−, CC, CO, CH, NH, and OH bonds, respectively, which proved the successful synthesis of the CDs-Ag NPs. Therefore, CDs-modified Ag NPs with plenty of COO−, OH, and NH2 groups can have interactions with metal ions.

To obtain a better knowledge about the synthesized CDs-Ag NPs, bare Ag NPs and CDs-Ag NPs were also analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS). As indicated in Fig. 3 (A), bare Ag NPs have an average hydrodynamic diameter of 10nm, whereas the CDs-Ag NPs show increased average hydrodynamic diameter of 25nm (Fig. 3B), confirming the assembly of CDs on the surface of Ag NPs.

The size and shape of the bare Ag NPs and CDs-Ag NPs were also examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The bare Ag NPs were spherical and well-dispersed with particle size of ∼7nm (Fig. 3C), whereas the average diameter of CDs-Ag NPs was ∼20nm

(Fig. 3D). As can be seen, TEM images were in good agreement with the DLS data, FT-IR analysis, and UV–vis spectroscopy measurement which altogether prove the successful formation of the CDs-Ag NPs with larger size than that of bare Ag NPs. Fortunately, it can be observed from the TEM images that the Ag NPs remained uniform and well-dispersed after modification with CDs which is desirable for a nanoparticle-based colorimetric sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing capability of CDs-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the spectral effects of various metal ions on aqueous dispersion of CDs-Ag NPs, the UV–vis spectrum of CDs-Ag NPs dispersion (3ml, 0.02mg/ml) was recorded in the presence of 10μM of each of the various metal ions, namely Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, K+, Na+, and Fe3+. As can be observed in Fig. 4 (A), among the studied metal ions, only Cu2+ ions caused a significant red-shift in SPR absorption band of CDs-Ag NPs. Fig. 4(B), illustrating photographic image of CDs-Ag NPs solutions containing 10μM of various metal ions, shows that except for the Cu2+ cations, other metal ions did not change the color of CDs-Ag NPs solution. The red-shift from 430 to 525nm in the SPR band, which led to the visual color change from orange to brown-red, can be attributed to the aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs which was triggered by the Cu2+ ions.

Fig. S1 shows that Cu2+ did not cause observable change on UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs. Also, no remarkable change in UV–vis absorption spectra of the bare Ag NPs (Fig. S2) was observed upon the addition of Cu2+, confirming the key role of the CDs in aggregation and color change of the CDs-Ag NPs by Cu2+ ions.

Since other metal ions did not induce considerable change in color and UV–vis spectra of CDs-Ag NPs in aqueous medium, Cu2+ could be selectively detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer and even by the naked eye using CDs-Ag NPs as a colorimetric sensor.

The outline of the mechanism of the developed sensor for Cu2+ is shown in Fig. 5 . The prepared CDs-Ag NPs were in the form of a stable and well-dispersed orange dispersion. The CDs participating in the structure of the nanocomposite were comprised of a carbon skeleton, edge- and surface-functionalized with a large number of oxygen-containing (OH and COO−) and nitrogen-containing (NH2) groups which can strongly interact with Cu2+ ions. It was anticipated that the NH2 groups would bind to the surface of the silver nanoparticles and the

OH and COO− groups would bring about the stabilization of CDs-Ag NPs in aqueous medium by means of repulsive electrostatic forces. It is a well-known fact that Cu2+ ions tend to interact with carboxylate and free amino groups via electrostatic interactions [43,44]. Therefore, owing to the formation of coordination complex between the Cu2+ ions and the aforementioned surface functional groups, CDs-Ag NPs were cross-linked through Cu2+ ions, which led to the aggregation of the CDs-Ag NPs with concomitant red shift of SPR absorption band from 430 to 525nm and color change of CDs-Ag NPs dispersion from orange to red-brown.

The TEM imaging and DLS analysis were also employed to prove the proposed mechanism. Fig. 6 (A) represents the TEM image of CDs-Ag NPs after the addition of 10μM Cu2+ indicating that the NPs had linked together and aggregated after interaction with Cu2+ cations. DLS measurement Fig. 6(B) also confirmed the aggregation by revealing that the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs-Ag NPs was increased to about 250nm in the presence of 10μM Cu2+.

First of all, the absorption properties of the prepared CDs-Ag NPs were studied at different pH levels in the range of 1–12 adjusted by aqueous solutions of HCl or NaOH. As shown in Fig. 7 , the SPR absorption peak of the CDs-Ag NPs was almost invariant in the pH range of 5–12. However, with decreasing pH to values <5, the SPR band at 430nm diminished and a new peak at 525nm emerged. These observations can be assigned to the protonation of carboxylate and amino groups at the acidic conditions. Protonation of the amino groups results in detachment of CDs from silver nanoparticles and aggregation of bare Ag NPs. On the other hand, protonation of carboxylate groups at CDs-Ag NPs surface results in diminishing the electrostatic repulsion between the NPs which further reinforces the self-aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs. Based on the obtained results, pH values >5 were found to be proper for the detection of Cu2+ by using CDs-Ag NPs as a colorimetric sensor.

To study the influence of pH on the analytical response of the proposed Cu2+ sensor, the pH of a number of identical CDs-Ag NPs dispersions containing 5μM Cu2+ were adjusted in the range of 5–12 by aqueous solution of HCl or NaOH. After a few minutes, when visual color change took place, UV–vis spectra of the solutions were recorded, and the absorption ratio values (A525/A430) were plotted versus the corresponding solution pH (Fig. S3). Considering the A525/A430 value, an effective interaction and aggregation was observed at 6 < pH < 9, which can be attributed to freedom of the surface amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylate functional groups from protonation, resulting in conditions more favorable for complex formation reactions between Cu2+ cations and the surface functional groups. This could, in turn, lead to the effective cupric-induced cross-linking and aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs. At pH < 6, A525/A430 values were less than those measured at 6 < pH < 9, which could be due to the partial protonation of the surface functional groups with consequent weaker interaction between protonated functional groups and Cu2+ cations resulting in less aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs. At more alkaline conditions (pH > 9), it is likely that the highly negatively charged CDs exerted increased repulsive forces which counteracted the interaction between cupric ions and the surface functional groups and prevented Ag NPs from approaching each other and aggregating. Moreover, at the more alkaline conditions, Cu2+ ions form Cu(OH)n that could not form any complexes with the surface functional groups. Therefore, the analytical response toward Cu2+ (i.e. A525/A430) decreased at pH > 9. Based on the above results, 6 < pH < 9 is useful for the detection of Cu2+ by the developed colorimetric sensor. Since de facto pH of the synthesized CDs-Ag NPs solution was 6.5, all experiments were carried out by the synthesized CDs-Ag NPs without addition of any pH adjusting agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs concentration used in CDs-Ag NPs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs prepared in this work possess many electron donating functional groups including amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylate which play an important role in the interaction between the CDs-Ag NPs and Cu2+. Accordingly, the amount of CDs used in the CDs-Ag NPs synthesis could have an important effect on both qualitative and quantitative aspects of the cupric-induced aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs and also on the analytical response of the proposed sensor toward Cu2+. In order to investigate the effect of CDs concentration, CDs-Ag NPs were synthesized with different amounts of CDs while the concentration of NaBH4 and AgNO3 were kept constant in all of the experiments. The diagram in Fig. S4 shows that when the CDs concentration was low, the cupric-induced aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs was weak and hence A525/A430 was low, reflecting the significant effect of CDs concentration on the aggregation-based response of the proposed sensor. The amount of the analytical signal (A525/A430) maximized when using 0.05mg/ml of CDs in synthesizing CDs-Ag NPs, but decreased with further increasing CDs concentration. Decrease in A525/A430 value at the higher amounts of CDs was probably due to the interaction of CDs remained free in the solution with Cu2+ cations which hampers cross-linking and aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs. Consequently, to get optimal results, CDs-Ag NPs prepared with 0.05mgml−1 of CDs was employed for the detection of Cu2+.

Optimum reaction time is an essential factor in colorimetric sensors [3]. In order to investigate the influence of the reaction time between CDs-Ag NPs and Cu2+ on the sensing process, the absorption of CDs-Ag NPs was measured at various elapsed times after the addition of three different concentrations of Cu2+, namely 2, 5, and 8μM. A525/A430 was recorded as a function of the time elapsed after mixing the reagents (Fig. S5). From the obtained results, it was deduced that the reaction could be completed at 10min. Therefore, all measurements were carried out 10min after mixing the sensing material with the analyte solution.

Ionic strength is one of the factors that may affect the results obtained from NPs-based colorimetric detections [21]. Thus, different amounts of NaCl were added to the solutions of CDs-Ag NPs containing 5μM Cu2+ and their UV–vis absorption were recorded after 10min. The A525/A430 values were plotted against NaCl concentration (Fig. S6). The results showed that neither positive nor negative effect on the analytical signal was observed over the range of 0–600μM NaCl. Therefore, the detection of Cu2+ can be performed at moderate ionic strengths by the developed sensor. Consequently, analyses were performed without adding any ionic strength buffer to the detection system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Cu2+ using CDs-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions mentioned above, CDs-Ag NPs was applied for the quantification of Cu2+ by using UV–vis spectrophotometry. In this manner, different amounts of standard Cu2+ were individually added into a series of 0.02mg/ml CDs-Ag NPs solutions and their UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded after 10min. Fig. 8 (A) shows that as the concentration of Cu2+ increased from 0.3 to 8μM, the SPR absorption peak at 430nm gradually attenuated, while in contrast, the absorption peak at 525nm drastically increased. In addition, with increasing the concentration of Cu2+, the color of CDs-Ag NPs dispersion changed from orange to red-brown (Fig. 8B) which was easily visible to the naked eye. The results indicated that the degree of CDs-Ag NPs aggregation was proportional to the concentration of Cu2+. Based on these findings, the calibration curve (Fig. 9 ) was established by plotting the absorption ratio, A525/A430, versus Cu2+ concentration within the range of 0.3–8μM. The calculated correlation coefficient of the calibration line was equal to r2 =0.9990. The linear regression equation of the calibration line was AR =0.1638C+0.3434, where AR and C designate the absorption ratio and concentration of Cu2+ (μM) respectively. The limit of detection (LOD=3sb/m) of the method was calculated to be 0.037μM which is much lower than the maximum admissible concentration (MAC) of Cu2+ in drinking water according to the World Health Organization (WHO), i.e. 31μM [45]. Table 1 represents a comparison between the performances of the proposed sensor with that of some other NPs-based colorimetric sensors (none of them include CDs-modified Ag NPs) reported previously. The precision of the method was studied by five replicate determinations of 2 and 5μM Cu2+ which resulted in percent relative standard deviations (RSD%) of 2.65% and 3.96%, respectively.

To study the selectivity of the proposed method, the tolerance concentration of various interfering ions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, K+, and Na+) in determination of Cu2+ (5μM) using the proposed colorimetric sensor were studied and the results are provided in Table 2 . The tolerance ratio was defined as the concentration ratio of interfering ions that generated a ±5% change in the analytical signal (A525/A430) of Cu2+. Based on the results obtained, the presence of even high concentration of these ions did not pose a problem in the determination of Cu2+ by the developed sensor. Nevertheless, only Hg2+ at high concentrations may influence the reliability and sensitivity of the proposed method and is thus needed to be masked or removed in order to not seriously affect the analytical results.

It is well-known that most of the studied metal cations can partially interact with carboxyl and amino groups at the surface of the CDs-Ag NPs and probably lead to the cross-linking and aggregation of these nanoparticles. However, the degree of aggregation depends on the strength of interaction as well as the size, charge, and steric compatibility of the cations towards the surface functional groups of CDs. According to the results obtained, although some cations such as Hg2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ caused the aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs at relatively high concentrations, Cu2+ ions could aggregate the CDs-Ag NPs more effectively than the other studied ions such that low concentrations of Cu2+ can be selectively determined by the proposed method without the serious interfering of the other cations.

In order to assess the applicability of the developed sensor, analysis of Cu2+ was carried out in real water samples by using CDs-Ag NPs. Tap, and river water samples were collected from local water sources. Prior to the analyses, the samples were filtered through a 0.45μm filter to remove any suspended particles. The samples were then analyzed by taking advantage of the standard addition method by spiking the sample with 0.7, 2, and 5μM of standard Cu2+ and by using the proposed sensor. The obtained results are summarized in Table 3 , indicating that the method has good recoveries ranged from 98% to 104%. The results confirmed that the developed colorimetric sensor could be used for determination of Cu2+ in real water samples with good accuracy and precision.

A new simple, sensitive, and selective colorimetric sensor for the detection and determination of Cu2+ was developed based on CDs-modified Ag NPs (CDs-Ag NPs). Investigation of UV–vis spectra, TEM images, and DLS analysis results of CDs-Ag NPs revealed that Cu2+ ions can effectively bring about the aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs, which was selectively promoted by the cupric ions. The aggregation was accompanied with a visible color change from orange to red-brown which was due to the significant wavelength shift in the SPR absorption peak. The LOD of Cu2+ with the naked eye and with UV–vis spectrophotometry was about 1μM and 0.037μM respectively. The obtained LODs are lower than the MAC of Cu+2 in drinking water according to the WHO standards. This indicates that the method not only can be used for the rapid visual detection and estimation of Cu2+ ions in water samples but also for the precise quantitative spectrophotometric determination of trace amounts of this cation in the same samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.06.049 .

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
